column3: The transcode-mcp is a stateless HTTP proxy. The URL is the API — /{media_type}/{options}/{source_url}. The proxy fetches the source, transforms it, caches the result, and returns it. No stored state for images (Cache API only). R2 content-addressed storage only for audio where Cloudflare lacks a binding with quality controls.
column4: Three successive simplifications from the initial ptxprint-mcp-clone model: (1) R2-folder-as-protocol, (2) Cloudflare Images binding discovered, (3) actual HTTP proxy — each round removed machinery. The final form has the fewest moving parts and zero orphan risk for images.
column5: ptxprint-mcp-clone architecture (Worker + Container + DOs + R2 for everything); batch processing API; stored variants in R2 for images

column2: Images: Cloudflare Images binding + Cache API
column3: Image transcoding uses the env.IMAGES Worker binding. The Worker fetches the source, gets dimensions via env.IMAGES.info(), computes half-class encode width, transforms via env.IMAGES.input().transform().output(), writes to Cache API, returns the response. No R2, no Container, no Durable Objects.
column4: Cloudflare Images binding does resize, quality, format conversion (AVIF/WebP/JPEG) — all from a ReadableStream within the Worker. Cache API provides deterministic URL-keyed caching with no orphan risk. 5,000 free transformations/month, $0.50/1,000 after.
column5: Container with ffmpeg for images; R2 storage of image variants; imgproxy/thumbor external service

column3: Audio transcoding requires a Container with ffmpeg because the Cloudflare Media Transformations binding (env.MEDIA) outputs audio as M4A/AAC with no bitrate, sample rate, or channel control. The voice/music preset craft requires ffmpeg codec flags. Outputs stored in R2 content-addressed by sha256(source_hash + normalized_params).
column4: The Media binding was verified (March 2026 launch, audio mode July 2025). It extracts audio from video as M4A but exposes only mode, time, duration, format — no quality/bitrate knobs. The borrow breaks at the point where the transcoding craft lives.
column5: Cloudflare Media binding for audio (insufficient controls); no audio support

column3: Three quality presets named low/medium/high, mapping to quality 20/50/80 for images. The wider spread (vs initial 40/60/80) is viable because the half-class upsample absorbs compression artifacts on the display downscale, making q=20 usable where it normally blocks up. Each preset maps to different codec settings per media type.
column4: Operator insight: with upsampling creating more variety in output, tighter spacing (40/60/80) would make presets too similar. 20/50/80 creates real differentiation and usefulness at each level.

column3: The proxy URL has three parts: media type (image/audio/video), comma-separated key=value options, and the source URL as the path tail. No URL-encoding of the source is needed. The media type routes the Worker before fetching and makes the URL self-documenting.
column4: Path-prefix without media type was rejected as too bespoke and not scalable for different option vocabularies per type. Query-params rejected because they require URL-encoding the source. The media type prefix is technically redundant with the source file extension but serves routing and readability.
column5: Query-param style (?src=...&w=...); path-prefix without media type; Cloudflare /cdn-cgi/image/ convention directly

column2: MCP tool is a URL constructor
column3: The MCP tool generates proxy URLs, not transcoded media. It takes source_url, media_type, and options, returns a URL string. The LLM picks the right preset and width based on context. The MCP layer is documentation-as-a-tool-surface — it teaches an LLM how to use the proxy.
column4: The primary caller is a translation workspace app (programmatic access). The MCP layer serves LLM assistants helping translators. Both use the same URLs. The proxy doesn't need to know which caller type is using it.
column5: MCP tool that fetches/transforms directly; batch orchestration tool

column3: The proxy handles one URL per request. Batch packaging (fetching many transcoded files and bundling them) is the caller's responsibility. The proxy doesn't know about batches. Each request is independent and independently cached.
column4: The translation app already has the list of media URLs for a pericope. It constructs proxy URLs by string concatenation and fetches them in parallel. The CDN handles the thundering herd naturally. Building batch infrastructure before anyone asks for it is premature machinery.
column5: Batch endpoint that accepts a list of URLs; packaging/zip service

column2: Cloudflare Images binding exists and fits
column3: Cloudflare provides env.IMAGES Worker binding that transforms images from a ReadableStream — resize, quality, format conversion to AVIF/WebP/JPEG — without needing a Container, R2, or public URL for the source. Discovered via prior-art search during this session. Available on all plans, 5,000 free transformations/month.
column4: Cloudflare docs, verified May 2026. The binding counts every call as a transformation (unlike URL-based transforms which deduplicate by uniqueness). Cache API in front of the binding prevents redundant billing.

column2: Cloudflare Media Transformations binding lacks audio quality controls
column3: The env.MEDIA binding (launched March 2026) can extract audio from video as M4A/AAC, but exposes only mode, time, duration, format — no bitrate, sample rate, or channel layout controls. This is sufficient for audio extraction but not for audio transcoding with quality presets. The borrow breaks here.
column4: Cloudflare docs for Media Transformations binding, verified May 2026. The binding is in public open beta with no billing during beta. Audio output is always AAC in M4A container.

column2: Stateless proxy eliminates orphan risk for images
column3: The image path stores nothing — no R2 objects, no database rows, no Durable Objects. The Cache API is the only persistence, managed entirely by Cloudflare with standard TTL eviction. There is nothing to orphan. The audio path has orphan risk (R2 objects), managed by R2 lifecycle rules (90-day eviction after last access).
column4: The orphan management concern was raised by the operator early in the session. Each architectural simplification reduced orphan surface. The final proxy model eliminates it entirely for the primary use case (images).

column2: Three successive simplifications driven by operator challenges
column3: The assistant's initial architecture (ptxprint-mcp clone) was simplified three times by operator challenges: (1) R2-folder-as-protocol removed explicit dispatch, (2) Cloudflare bindings removed the Container for images, (3) proxy model removed stored state entirely. Each round removed machinery. The borrow-don't-build discipline works at the architecture level, not just the recipe level.
column4: This is the same pattern as the exploration session's three assistant corrections — the operator's challenges consistently produced better engineering than the assistant's first answer.

column2: Primary use case: offline Bible translation media packaging
column3: The primary use case is packaging media resources (images and audio) attached to a pericope assignment in a Bible translation project, optimized for offline use on a cheap Android phone with limited storage and a slow connection. The translation workspace app constructs proxy URLs and bundles the results.
column4: Operator-stated use case. Confirms q=20 as the most impactful preset (most constrained target), the cheap-Android target chain from exploration, and why batch is the caller's concern (the app already knows the media list).

column2: Create the GitHub repo and push initial commit
column3: The operator created the public repo github.com/klappy/transcode-mcp. This handoff populates it with the initial commit.

column5: For audio, the Worker must download-and-hash the source before cache-checking R2. For images, the Cache API keys by URL (no content hashing needed), so this open item is now audio-only.

column4: Streaming video transcode DO state machine
column5: Unchanged from exploration. Deferred with video from v1.
For audio, the Container profiles the source within a single job. Cross-target reuse is architecturally sound but undecided.

column4: Image quality-floor metric deferred to v2
column5: Unchanged from exploration. V1 uses quality presets (20/50/80).
Exact formula with worked examples for edge cases. The principle is clear; the exact arithmetic needs a focused pass.
The ffmpeg flags for each preset+quality combination as a data file. Verify-then-adopt at pick-time.
